author: @ZsOtherBrother
text:
Doing things because they're interesting and fun, and not necessarily because they are profitable or efficient or practical, seems like a good way to experience life in general. 
Trustworthiness is priceless!
Validation and proof of concept are an important step, and the fact that you're willing to share that step with us should not be taken for granted.
You owe us nothing, and we have no right to expect anything more than what you're willing to share with us... and you share quite a lot.
Scaling things up, and developing useful applications, is up to those of us with that inclination.
We can ask nicely, (a circuit diagram where electronics are involved would help me understand some things better:)), but other than that we should be thankful for what we receive, and make the best of it.
I'm sure many feel the same way, but I can only speak for myself, so I thank you for bringing "stuff" to my attention in such an informative and entertaining way.

author: @DimitriPappas
text: With regards to the scaling aspect, I think that while a lot of people (myself included) truly appreciate your DIY take on doing these things, there seems to be a lack of information (especially with regards to electric wind power generators) that bridges the gap between a working concept/model as you put it, and something that is commercially viable. What I mean by that is, there are a lot of us who are deterred by the exorbitant prices usually associated with wind turbines on the market, and, knowing that there are options out there (ie, repurposed, low-rpm permanent magnet DC motors from the likes of hover boards, e-bikes, and treadmills), seem to be struggling to find any meaningful resources that give a proper clear direction or comparison of these options, yet our desire/will to build not just a "conceptual" prototype, but a viable energy producing (say, 100-300w nominal) wind generator for home use, without blowing loads of time, money/resources into research and prototyping, still stands. You might be thinking "well I'm focusing on the conceptual side of the content, why should I focus on the scaling too, that's more work? Besides, that's a void of opportunity for another youtube creator to capture and focus on.", and you'd be 100% correct. But your channel, while it focuses more on the curious, conceptual aspects of these technologies, happens to be one of the few that shows any glimmer of hope/promise to the desperate viewer right now, of ever exploring some kind of scaled up models in this regard, but is yet to actually do so. I think this observation lies at the root of why some people expect more from you, or why you might get some hate comments for not disclosing enough information about the technical specifics even at your small scale, which your viewers could extrapolate on / use as a point of reference. Anyways I personally treasure channels like yours and am appreciative of what you have done to contribute, regardless, and my last intention would be to try impose further expectations on you in that regard. What sort of content you produce and the depth that you go into it, is entirely your own discretion that's how it should be. I think this is just one of the negative side effects of being a YouTuber in general - people feel entitled to a creator's content despite it being free to them, oblivious to the amount of work and effort that actually goes into producing such videos. But it is part and parcel regardless - inevitable that you will receive such a diversity of comments. Just some insights from another viewer's perspective. Keep the gems coming ;)

author: @PressToDigitate
text: Robert, don't get tied down by the Lilliputians!  Lots of silly people will toss out inane criticisms and stupid negative comments.  Pay it no mind.  You're an *outstanding* researcher, experimentalist and educator (and, theoretician, as well, I would imagine).  The only thing holding you back is sorting out which problem you really need to solve, to both "do good" and "do well" in the doing of it, at the same time.  Here's one you might have a go at (that you could do on the benchtop): *A Sodium Carbonate/Hydrochloric Acid Flow Battery/Fuel Cell* .  Schemes for Atmospheric Carbon Capture generally rely on the absorption of CO2 into a Sodium Hydroxide solution (producing Sodium Carbonates); its cheap, reliable, and in industrial use for many years already.  Typically, either the Sodium Carbonate is to be sequestered as mineral solids, or, where CO2 recovery is desired (as for the production of Algae Based Biofuels), it must be *heated* to release the CO2 into a controlled system.  A particular energy system is possible which produces HCl as a *byproduct* , which would be available to liberate the CO2 from Sodium Carbonate solution without the input of thermal energy (regenerating the NaOH) - *BUT, it is a dynamic, exothermic reaction from which an electric current should be derivable directly* - IF someone worked out what *electrodes* and configuration might best accomplish that.  If you had unlimited streams of Sodium Carbonate solution and Hydrochloric Acid (both at 'some' levels of dilution, TBD), and you could put a "Toll Booth" on those streams to extract energy, how might you go about it?

author: @johnhenson8862
text:
The wind belt description in Wiki provided some numbers INCLUDING cost. $50-125 per Watt. How awesome! Wiki also advises that an industrial scale wind turbine is USD 1.3m per MW  or USD 1.30 per watt.   The efficiencies for both are also provided.  So now what?
A researcher would pause and wonder how to knock of at least an order of magnitude off the Wind Belt (as Einstein would say - thought experiments, look up Wiki for  "Einstein thought experiments") BEFORE engaging in playing inventor.  No need to be Einstein, just use his methods. 
At a minimum, a researcher would pause to discover key issues before starting their journey of discovery, all with an eye of making the device of utility to everyone using the popular metrics of efficiency,  cost and performance. And guess what - just thinking about something may give a break through at zero cost, or tempt physical experiment that does cost, fails but during the whole process something interesting may have been learned - and it is amazing how years later that "failure"   provides useful insight into a completely different problem.
All the above is akin to a carpenter, who from being an apprentice to master not only builds furniture, but  also spends his career building his physical tools e.g. workbench, chocks etc.  On the RM-S site it is about building the mental tools, or memories of experimentation. A disciplined process.

author: @SheplerStudios
text:
I’ve learned so much from your channel and deeply appreciate what you’re trying to do, Robert. Here in the United States, the Republican party and religious Conservatives have fundamentally declared a war on science and seek to dumb down this nation, sacrificing longterm economic stability for short terms gains in political power. Encouraging science, and critical thinking to mitigate such growing environmental catastrophes like Climate Change is discourage by the far-Right in America, as if intellectual curiosity and any general scientific consensus are some grand liberal conspiracy to overcome.

Astrophysicist Carl Sagan wrote in “The Demon-Haunted World: Science as a Candle in the Dark — The dumbing down of American is most evident in the slow decay of substantive content in the enormously influential media, the 30 second sound bites (now down to 10 seconds or less), lowest common denominator programming, credulous presentations on pseudoscience and superstition, but especially a kind of celebration of ignorance”

Thanks for being one of those candles against the growing darkness in the world.

Rob!

My bench has become acluttered 
with bits of carbon rod and wire
old galvanised iron with paste abuttered
and strange things tinged by fire.

my fingers burned by flux.
Permanganate stains my coffee mugs
My circuitry - it sucks!

My work-flow has gone assunder
Yet my note books overflow
What becomes of my latest blunder
The world will never know.

Yet each day or so you refresh my soul
And to the shed I go
To retrieve some clay from a distant hole
and to happ'ly go with the flow.

So thanks to you RM-S, 
For your frequent inspiration
Someday, betweeen us, I confess
We will somehow save the nation!

replies.author: @robmarrin6720
replies.text: @SinsBird dry solder joints are very common and usually take a while to manifest the fault, sometimes it could be a capacitor blown or even a crafty internal fuse like old hifi's but if it's broken you can't really break it any more than it is so take a chance it'll be either fixed or not 👍❤️

replies.author: @sydsykes8354
replies.text:
In my case there were four burned out contacts on the back of the circuit board directly beneath the four relays, only two relays were affected. 
I first re soldered these but it still didnt work.
Stripped it again cut the tops off all four relays so that I could see the contact "posts" put the multimeter onto the contact post inside the relay and on the reverse of the circuit board to check that there was contact between the circuit board and the relay and in each case there was.
Then I put the multimeter on the contact post and the arm inside the relay that clicks in when activate to see if that was contacting with the contact post and it was in three of the relays but not on the fourth one.
I looked at the contact points and they were spotless so I examined the moving arm and noticed that there was a spring like strip behind it that helped either push it closed or opened it up so I carefully bent it in the direction that I estimated it would help the points close more firmly.
Re assembled it and couldn't stop smiling.
Still cannot even now 😄😄

replies.author: @sydsykes8354
replies.text:
One point I would add is that I had nothing to lose as the inverter was not working so I couldn't make it any worse.
That made me a little braver
